title: Entropy-Guided Adaptive Token Merging for Robust and Efficient Vision Transformers
description: We propose EGTM (Entropy-Guided Token Merging), a training-free method that adapts the token merging ratio in Vision Transformers based on per-layer CLS-row attention entropy. Under clean inputs where attention is sharp, EGTM merges aggressively for efficiency; under corrupted or shifted inputs where attention becomes diffuse, it reduces merging to preserve accuracy. This addresses the previously unstudied problem of token reduction robustness under distribution shift.
motivation: Token reduction methods (ToMe, EViT, etc.) are widely used to speed up Vision Transformers at inference time, but they have never been evaluated under distribution shift. Recent adaptive token merging methods (AToM, AdapTiV) optimize merging for efficiency on clean data but remain agnostic to input degradation. Training-free robustness methods like SATA (Nikzad et al., CVPR 2025) improve ViT robustness through spatial token analysis but do not address how token merging itself degrades under corruption. In real-world deployment (autonomous driving, medical imaging, edge devices), both efficiency AND robustness are needed simultaneously. We hypothesize that token merging degrades disproportionately under corruption because: (1) the key cosine similarity used for bipartite matching is distorted when input perturbations propagate through key projections, and (2) the fixed merging ratio fails to account for input difficulty. This is supported by Guo et al. (ICCV 2023) who showed corruption causes highly diverging attention patterns — exactly the structure token merging relies on.
proposed_approach: EGTM modifies the token merging pipeline with one core training-free component: Entropy-Adaptive Merging Ratio using a CLS-row attention entropy proxy. We compute the entropy of the CLS token's attention distribution (1xN per head, negligible overhead) at inference time and compare it to calibration statistics from clean data. When entropy exceeds the normal range (indicating distribution shift), we exponentially reduce the merging ratio using r_l = r_0 * max(alpha, exp(-beta * delta_l)). The CLS-row proxy avoids materializing the full NxN attention matrix, adding <1% overhead vs. the full-matrix approach. An optional enhancement — importance-aware token protection — is evaluated via ablation. The method is a drop-in replacement for ToMe that works with any pretrained ViT.

related_work.title: Token Merging: Your ViT But Faster
related_work.authors: Daniel Bolya, Cheng-Yang Fu, Xiaoliang Dai, Peizhao Zhang, Christoph Feichtenhofer, Judy Hoffman
related_work.relevance: The primary method we build upon. ToMe uses bipartite soft matching on key cosine similarity to merge tokens with a fixed ratio. Evaluated only on clean data; we study and fix its behavior under distribution shift.

related_work.title: SATA: Spatial Autocorrelation Token Analysis for Enhancing the Robustness of Vision Transformers
related_work.authors: Nick Nikzad, Yi Liao, Yongsheng Gao, Jun Zhou
related_work.relevance: Training-free robustness method using Moran's I spatial autocorrelation to group tokens before FFN. Directly comparable baseline: also training-free and targets ViT robustness. However, SATA modifies FFN computation via spatial grouping, while we address degradation of token MERGING decisions. The approaches are complementary.

related_work.title: Token Reduction Should Go Beyond Efficiency in Generative Models — From Vision, Language to Multimodality
related_work.authors: Zhenglun Kong, Yize Li, Fanhu Zeng, Lei Xin, Shvat Messica, Xue Lin, Pu Zhao, Manolis Kellis, Hao Tang, Marinka Zitnik
related_work.relevance: Survey arguing token reduction transcends efficiency, impacting robustness, alignment, and stability. Our work directly instantiates this vision by showing token reduction impacts robustness under distribution shift.

related_work.title: AToM: Adaptive Token Merging for Efficient Acceleration of Vision Transformer
related_work.authors: J. Shin, M. Kang, Y. Han, J. Park, L.-S. Kim
related_work.relevance: Image-adaptive fine-grained token merging with algorithm-architecture co-design. Adapts WHICH tokens to merge for efficiency on clean data; does not study robustness under distribution shift.

related_work.title: AdapTiV: Sign-Similarity Based Image-Adaptive Token Merging for Vision Transformer Acceleration
related_work.authors: Seungjae Yoo, Hangyeol Kim, Joo-Young Kim
related_work.relevance: Sign-similarity based image-adaptive token merging with hardware co-design. Focuses on efficiency optimization; does not study robustness under corrupted inputs.

related_work.title: Entropy-Guided Condensing for Vision Transformer
related_work.authors: Shangqun Lin, Peng Lyu, Dongliang Liu et al.
related_work.relevance: Uses transfer entropy to condense ViT LAYERS (depth-wise reduction) via training-time Dilution Learning. Different mechanism (transfer entropy vs. CLS attention entropy), different target (layers vs. tokens), different setting (training-time vs. inference-time).

related_work.title: Robustifying Token Attention for Vision Transformers
related_work.authors: Yong Guo, David Stutz, Bernt Schiele
related_work.relevance: Identifies the token overfocusing problem in ViTs under corruption — attention concentrates on few non-robust tokens with highly diverging patterns. Provides key empirical evidence supporting our hypothesis that attention structure (and thus token merging quality) degrades under corruption.

related_work.title: Benchmarking Neural Network Robustness to Common Corruptions and Perturbations
related_work.relevance: Establishes the ImageNet-C benchmark and mean Corruption Error (mCE) metric that we use for systematic robustness evaluation.

related_work.title: Stabilizing Transformer Training by Preventing Attention Entropy Collapse
related_work.authors: Shuangfei Zhai, Tatiana Likhomanenko, Etai Littwin, Dan Busbridge, Jason Ramapuram, Yizhe Zhang, Jiatao Gu, Joshua M. Susskind
related_work.relevance: Established attention entropy as a meaningful diagnostic of transformer behavior. We use attention entropy in a complementary way: as an inference-time signal for distribution shift detection rather than a training stability metric.

related_work.title: Delving Deep into the Generalization of Vision Transformers under Distribution Shifts
related_work.relevance: Shows ViTs have stronger shape bias and generalize better than CNNs under shift. Provides foundation for understanding ViT robustness but does not study efficient ViTs.

related_work.title: Not All Patches are What You Need: Expediting Vision Transformers via Token Reorganizations
related_work.authors: Youwei Liang, Chongjian Ge, Zhan Tong, Yibing Song, Jue Wang, Pengtao Xie
related_work.relevance: EViT keeps top-k attentive tokens and fuses the rest. Requires fine-tuning; we evaluate a training-free variant for fair comparison.

related_work.title: DynamicViT: Efficient Vision Transformers with Dynamic Token Sparsification
related_work.authors: Yongming Rao, Wenliang Zhao, Benlin Liu, Jiwen Lu, Jie Zhou, Cho-Jui Hsieh
related_work.relevance: Learns token importance prediction modules for dynamic pruning. Requires training; representative of training-based token reduction.

related_work.title: Training data-efficient image transformers & distillation through attention
related_work.authors: Hugo Touvron, Matthieu Cord, Matthijs Douze, Francisco Massa, Alexandre Sablayrolles, Herve Jegou
related_work.relevance: DeiT is our primary backbone architecture. Pretrained DeiT-S and DeiT-B are used in all experiments.

related_work.title: Is Less More? Exploring Token Condensation as Training-free Test-time Adaptation
related_work.authors: Zixin Wang, Dong Gong, Sen Wang, Zi Huang, Yadan Luo
related_work.relevance: Uses token condensation for TTA in VLMs (CLIP). Complementary: focuses on adaptation rather than making token reduction itself robust.

hypothesis: Standard token merging methods (ToMe) suffer disproportionate accuracy degradation under distribution shift compared to uncompressed ViTs (supported by the finding that corruption disrupts attention patterns used for merging), and an entropy-guided adaptive merging strategy using CLS-row attention entropy can recover >50% of this excess degradation while maintaining >80% of the throughput improvement on clean images.
success_criteria: ToMe shows statistically significant disproportionate accuracy drop under distribution shift vs. no-reduction baseline (relative accuracy drop gap > 2%)
EGTM reduces the disproportionate degradation by >50% compared to standard ToMe on ImageNet-C
EGTM maintains >80% of ToMe's throughput improvement on clean images
Results are consistent across both DeiT-S and DeiT-B
CLS-row entropy is a sufficient proxy for full-matrix entropy with <5% overhead
Ablation confirms adaptive ratio is the primary contributor to robustness gains
Attention entropy correlates positively with corruption severity (Spearman rho > 0.5) for majority (>10/15) of corruption types
Failure modes (entropy-decrease corruptions) are identified and reported transparently

breakdown.calibration: 0.5 hours — compute CLS-row entropy statistics on clean validation subset
breakdown.main_benchmark: 3.5 hours — 2 models x 6 methods (incl. SATA) on ImageNet val + ImageNet-C + ImageNet-R
breakdown.ablations: 1.5 hours — CLS-row vs. full entropy, hyperparameter sweep on DeiT-S with 3 corruptions
breakdown.efficiency_and_analysis: 1.0 hour — throughput measurements, entropy overhead, visualizations, correlation analysis

subfield: efficient vision transformers, robustness, distribution shift
contribution_type: First systematic study of token reduction robustness under distribution shift
Novel entropy-guided adaptive token merging method (training-free) with efficient CLS-row entropy proxy
Comprehensive benchmark comparing token reduction methods on corruption and shift datasets, including SATA as a training-free robustness baseline
Analysis of failure modes where entropy-based shift detection may fail
